pages.text: Crack classiﬁcation in concrete based on acoustic emission Kentaro Ohno a,*, Masayasu Ohtsu b,1 a Dept. of Civil & Environmental Engineering, Tokyo Metropolitan University, 1-1, Minamiohsawa, Hachioji, Tokyo 192-0397, Japan b Graduate School of Science and Technology, Kumamoto University, 2-39-1, Kurokami, Kumamoto 860-8555, Japan article info Article history: Available online 8 June 2010 Keywords: Acoustic emission Concrete Crack classiﬁcation SiGMA analysis Parameter analysis abstract The fracture mode of cracking in concrete is normally changing from tensile mode to shear mode at impending failure. As for crack classiﬁcation in concrete by acoustic emission (AE) techniques, two crack classiﬁcation methods have been carried out. One of them is parameter-based method (parameter anal- ysis) which has been carried out by employing two parameters of the average frequency and the RA value. The proportion of these two parameters, however, has not been determined yet. The other crack classiﬁcation is simpliﬁed Green’s functions for moment tensor analysis (SiGMA) procedure which is known as signal-based method. The SiGMA analysis is based on the generalized theory of AE, while the parameter analysis results from an empirical relation. Therefore, an optimal proportion of the param- eter analysis is in great demand. In this study, these crack classiﬁcation methods are compared and dis- cussed from results of three types of concrete failure tests. As a result, ratios of the shear crack which are identiﬁed by SiGMA analysis are good agreement with those by parameter analysis in the case that the proportion of the RA value and the average frequency is set to 1–200. Crown Copyright /C2112010 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction The number of aged concrete structures has been increasing all over the world. Since cracks are a major cause of damage in rein- forced concrete (RC) structures, the estimation methods for crack distribution are in great demand. To make a decision on mainte- nance and repair in concrete structure, non-destructive evaluation (NDE) techniques are often applied to estimate the safety and the performance of current state of concrete structures. Acoustic emis- sion (AE) technique which is one of NDE techniques is a useful method for the investigation of local damage in concrete. Thus, AE techniques have been applied to crack detection in concrete structures [1–3]. The damage degree in concrete structures can be easily estimated, taking account of the number of AE hits and maximum amplitude of AE signal [4,5]. In addition, AE source localization due to micro-cracks can be performed by taking into account arrival time differences of AE waveforms at each AE sensor [6,7]. Fracture and failure mechanisms in concrete are identiﬁed by introducing the moment tensor analysis of AE signal-based method [6–9]. One powerful technique for the moment tensor analysis has been developed as simpliﬁed Green’s functions for moment tensor analysis (SiGMA) procedure by which crack kinematics of loca- tions, crack types and orientations are quantitatively determined [10]. The SiGMA analysis is successfully applied to fracture tests of RC specimens under four-point bending to visualize the cracking progress [11,12]. According to results of the SiGMA analysis, it is known in the process of concrete fracture that cracks of tensile type would be mainly obtained in the early stage, then mixed- mode cracks of tensile and shear type

2. Theory of crack classiﬁcation by AE 2.1. AE parameter analysis AE signals due to micro-cracks are detected by AE sensors at- tached on the surface of the concrete specimen. Such some AE parameters as number of hits, count, duration time, amplitude, en- ergy, and rise time as shown in Fig. 1 are recorded by AE measure- ment system. In order to classify active cracks, AE parameters of the rise time and the maximum amplitude are applied to calculate RA value, and the average frequency is obtained from AE count and the duration time as, RA value ¼ the rise time =the maximum amplitude ; ð1Þ The average frequency ¼ AE ringdown-count =the duration time : ð2Þ From these two parameters, cracks are readily classiﬁed into tensile and shear cracks as illustrated in Fig. 2 . This crack classiﬁ- cation method is based on the JCMS-III B5706 code, of which re- sults were conﬁrmed under the four-point bending tests and the direct shear tests of concrete specimens. However, a deﬁned crite- rion on the proportion of the RA value and the average frequency for crack classiﬁcation has not been conﬁrmed. 2.2. SiGMA analysis According to the generalized theory of AE [15], detected AE sig- nals are generated by dynamic-crack (dislocation) motions inside a solid. The SiGMA analysis which consists of AE source location pro- cedure and moment tensor analysis of AE source has been devel- oped by based on the generalized theory of AE. Two parameters of the arrival time (P1) and the amplitude of ﬁrst motion (P2) as shown in Fig. 3 are applied to the analysis. So far, the determina- tion of these parameters has been carried out by visual and hand-picking, it requires time and energy for enormous number of AE waveforms for each concrete failure test. Therefore, auto- mated detection method for the ﬁrst motion in AE waveforms is in great demand. Ohno and Ohtsu developed the automated detec- tion method based on Akaike Information Criteria (AIC) [17], this technique is applied to the analysis. In the AE source location procedure, AE source location y is determined from the arrival time differences ti between the obser- vation point xi and xi+1, by solving equations Ri /C0 Riþ1 ¼j xi /C0 yj/C0j xiþ1 /C0 yj¼ vpti: ð3Þ Here, R is distance between the source y and the observation point x and vp is the velocity of P-wave. After determining the AE source location, parameters P2 at each sensor are substituted into the left hand of the following equation: AðxÞ¼ CS Refðt; cÞ R /C1 cpcqMpq /C1 DA: ð4Þ Here, A(x) is the amplitude of the ﬁrst motion (P2) and CS is the cal- ibration coefﬁcient of the sensor sensitivity and material constants. The reﬂection coefﬁcient Ref ( t, c) is obtained as t is the direction of sensor sensitivity. DA is area of crack surface, Mpq is the moment tensor and c is the direction vector of distance R from the source y to the observation point x as shown in Fig. 4 . Following the AE source location procedure, all quantities in Eq. (4) are known expect for moment tensor components. In an isotropic material, the mo- ment tensor is symmetric and of the second order, the number of unknown moment tensor components is six. Here, from the elasto- dynamic, heterogeneity is dependent on the relation between the wave-length and the size of heterogeneity (aggregate in concrete). In the case that the wave-lengths are larger than the sizes of aggre- gates, the effect of heterogeneous becomes minor [9]. Since P-wave velocity in concrete is about 4000 m/s, wave-leng

are determined from the amplitudes of the ﬁrst motions at more than six observation point. In order to classify crack type, the eigenvalue analysis of the moment tensor was developed [10]. In general, the eigenvalues of the moment tensor are represented by the combination of the shear crack (slip motion) and the tensile crack (crack-opening mo- tion). The following decomposition is obtained as the relative ra- tios X, Y and Z, 1:0 ¼ X þ Y þ Z; The intermediate eigenvalue =the maximum eigenvalue ¼ 0 /C0 Y=2 þ Z; The minimum eigenvalue =the maximum eigenvalue ¼/C0 X /C0 Y=2 þ Z; ð5Þ where X is the shear component, Y is the deviatoric tensile compo- nent and Z is the hydrostatic mean of tensile. In the present SiGMA code, crack is classiﬁed AE source with X > 60% as a shear crack, one with X < 40% as a tensile crack and one with 40% < X < 60% as a mixed-mode crack. The crack-motion vector l and the normal vector n to the crack surface are determined from three eigenvectors e1, e2 and e3. e1 ¼ l þ n e2 ¼ l /C2 n e3 ¼ l /C0 n ð6Þ To visualize these kinematical information of AE sources, Light Wave 3D software (New Tek) is introduced. Crack modes of tensile, mixed-mode and shear are given in Fig. 5 . Here, an arrow vector indicates a crack-motion vector, and a circular plate corresponds to a crack surface, which is perpendicular to a crack normal vector. 3. Experiment procedure In order to compare the results of the crack classiﬁcation meth- ods of parameter analysis and SiGMA analysis, three types of con- crete failure tests were conducted. Same condition of AE measurement was applied to all tests. Mix proportion of concrete for all failure tests are summarized in Table 1. AE signals were de- tected by eight AE sensors of 150 kHz resonance (R15, PAC) and sampling frequency for recording waveforms was 1 MHz by 8- channel DiSP system (PAC). AE waves were ampliﬁed with 40 dB gain by pre-ampliﬁer and 20 dB gain by DiSP system. AE sensors were attached to the surface of the specimen with an electron wax, so that the sensor face and the specimen surface have a good contact for the signal detection. Three types of failure experiments were tested under the same threshold levels of 42 dB. 3.1. Four-point bending test of normal concrete Since the crack classiﬁcation method by parameter analysis based on the JCMS-III B5706 code results from four-point bending tests, AE measurement were carried out to normal concrete under the four-point bending. Prism specimens of dimensions 100 mm /C2 100 mm /C2 400 mm and the conﬁguration of AE sensors are shown in Fig. 6 . 3.2. Four-point bending test of RC beam Many RC structures have been severely damaged by huge earth- quakes. In particular, the diagonal-shear failure in RC beams could lead to serious damage. Thus, experimental works have been con- ducted, aimed at studying the mechanisms of the diagonal-shear failure in RC members [12]. RC beam of dimensions Tensile Crack Mixed-mode Crack Shear Crack Tensile Crack Mixed-mode Crack Shear Crack Fig. 5. Display models for tensile crack, mixed-mode crack and shear crack. 150 mm /C2 250 mm /C2 2000 mm with 400 mm shear span was de- signed as the ratio of a shear span to an effective depth was set to 1.97. The specimen is illustrated in Fig. 7 along with arrange- ment of reinforcement. Two reinforcing-steel bars of 13 mm diam- eter (D13) and 10 mm diameter of two reinforcing-steel bars (D10) were employed. Generally, in order to increase shear capacity of RC beam, stir-rups are arranged in the shear span. No stir-rup rein- forcement, however, was arranged in the right-hand shear span as shown in Fig. 7, as the diagonal-shear failure could be localized. To monitor only the diagonal-shear failure in the RC beam with AE sensors, AE sensors were attached to the surface of RC beam spec- imen in the target shear span. Optimal array of AE sensors as shown in Fig. 8 were determined from numerical experiments [12]. AE signals were detected when amplitude was higher than threshold level of 42 dB. 3.3. Hydrostatic expansion test of concrete Corrosion of reinforcing-steel bar (rebar) is known to be one of critical issue in RC structures. Until now, a variety of studies for corrosion-induced cracks have been conducted on NDE. By apply- ing AE techniques, it has been reported that process of corrosion- induced cracks in concrete can be effectively estimated [18].I n addition, mechanisms of corrosion-induced cracks in concrete have been studied not only experimentally but also numerically by applying AE measurement and the boundary element method (BEM) [19,20]. In this study, to simulate cracks due to rebar corrosion, an expansive pressure was artiﬁcially introduced by employing an 100100100100 500 2000 004005 250 5046.5 77 150 1000 D10 D13 Fig. 7. expansive agent. Specimens of dimensions 250 mm /C2 250 mm /C2 150 mm with a hole of 30 mm diameter were designed. The hole was created at location of rebar with 20 mm of cover-thickness from surface as shown in Fig. 9. The expansive agent was cast into a hole in a specimen, and then AE monitoring started and also the expansive pressure was measured by using an embedded pressure- gauge. 4. Results and discussion 4.1. Four-point bending test of normal concrete According to JCMS-III code, parameter analysis is carried out by moving average of the RA value and the average frequency based on over 50 AE hits. Fig. 10 shows the results of the parameter anal- ysis based on JCMS-III code for detected all AE hits. In this study, 100 AE hits are applied to the parameter of moving average. In addition, Fig. 11 shows temporal change of the parameter analysis. It is found that the average frequency becomes low, while high RA value is obtained at ﬁnal moment. In the case that the proportion of the RA value and the average frequency is set to 1:80 as shown in Fig. 10 , the ratio of tensile crack becomes 78.6% and 21.4% AE hits are classiﬁed as shear crack. Detected AE waveforms are applied to the SiGMA analysis. As a result, 118 AE events are determined, such crack kinematics as locations, crack type and orientations are illustrated in Fig. 12 .A s seen in Fig. 12 , a lot of tensile cracks are observed at bottom of the specimen and tensile cracks are dominantly obtained at ﬁnal moment as shown in Fig. 13 . From results of the SiGMA analysis, 44.9% AE events for all 118 AE events are classiﬁed as tensile cracks, 23.7% AE events are classiﬁed as mixed-mode cracks, and the ratio of shear cracks becomes 31.4%. Here, comparing results of the parameter analysis with those of the SiGMA analysis, the ratios of the tensile cracks and shear cracks by parameter analysis are not agreement with those of the SiGMA analysis. The difference between crack classiﬁcation results of the parameter analysis and those of the SiGMA analysis could result from the fact that the parameter analysis is carried out for all AE signals, while the SiGMA analysis is applied to only AE events. Thus, to compare properly between results of the parameter anal- ysis and those of the SiGMA analysis, AE signals of 118 AE events are selected from all AE hits data for the parameter analysis. In addition, since ﬁrst arrival signal in an AE event is not relatively inﬂuenced due to attenuation of AE signal than the others, ﬁrst ar- rival signals are selected from 118 AE events data sets. The relation between the RA value and the average frequency is shown in Fig. 12. Fig. 14. Here, to compare the results of the parameter analysis with those of the SiGMA analysis, mixed-mode cracks are assumed to belong to tensile cracks in the SiGMA analysis. As a result, the ra- tios of the tensile crack and shear crack in parameter analysis are good agreement with those of the SiGMA analysis as shown in Ta- ble 2 in the case that the proportion of the RA value and the aver- age frequency is set to 1–200. 4.2. Four-point bending test of RC beam Since failure mechanisms in diagonal-shear failure are different from four-point bending of normal concrete, characteristics of de- tected AE signals in diagonal-shear failure are thought to be differ- ent from those in four-point bending of normal concrete. Figs. 15 and 16 show the results of the SiGMA analysis in the diagonal- shear failure of RC beam. The ratios of tensile cracks and mixed- mode cracks are 25.14% and 19.73%, respectively. 55.14% AE events are classiﬁed as shear cracks. In the diagonal-shear failure, domi- nant motions of micro-cracks are shear cracks, while tensile cracks are mainly obtained at ﬁnal moment in bending test. It is realized that the difference between failure process of the diagonal-shear failure and those of bending failure are clearly obtained in the view of micro-cracks by the SiGMA analysis. Fig. 15. Based on results of the parameter analysis in bending test, the relation between the RA value and the average frequency in diag- onal-shear failure are given in Fig. 17 . As for crack classiﬁcation, good agreement between results of the SiGMA analysis and the parameter analysis are conﬁrmed as shown in Table 3 . 4.3. Hydrostatic expansion test The results of the SiGMA analysis in hydrostatic expansion test are shown in Figs. 18 and 19 . It is found that generation of tensile cracks and mixed-mode cracks is earlier ceased than those of shear cracks as given in Fig. 20. In the case that the proportion of the RA value and the average frequency is set to 1–200 in parameter anal- ysis for crack classiﬁcation, it is found that results of the parameter analysis and those of SiGMA analysis are almost similar as shown in Table 4 . 5. Conclusion To evaluate properly crack and degradation in concrete struc- tures by AE techniques, two crack classiﬁcation methods of the parameter analysis and the SiGMA analysis are discussed. In or- der to clarify the relationship between these two crack classiﬁca- tion methods, AE monitoring was applied to three types of concrete failure tests. The following conclusions are derived: (1) Cracking mechanisms of all three concrete failure are deter- mined as AE cluster by SiGMA analysis. It is found that ten- sile cracks generated mainly in the bending test, in constant, shear cracks occurs dominantly in the diagonal-shear failure and hydrostatic expansion test. It is suggested that domi- nant crack motion might be changed due to the difference of failure process. (2) Moving average of the RA value and the average frequency are applied to the crack classiﬁcation based on the JCMS-III code. As a result, the ratios of tensile crack and shear crack by SiGMA analysis are different from those by the parameter analysis. (3) In the case that the proportion of the RA value and the aver- age frequency is set to 1–200 for ﬁrst detected AE signal in an AE event, the ratios of the tensile cracks and shear cracks in the SiGMA analysis are good agreement with those of the parameter analysis, regardless of the difference of the failure process. Here, by assuming that mixed-mode cracks belong to the tensile cracks in the SiGMA analysis, results of two crack classiﬁcation methods are comparable. Thus, it is sug- gested that the parameters of the ﬁrst arrival signal in an AE event are included most important information on crack generation than the others. References [1] Ohtsu M.
